title: Synthesis of mono-6-tosyl-β-cyclodextrin, a key intermediate for the functional cyclodextrin derivatives
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"ljk4t","text":"Mono-6-tosyl-β-cyclodextrin (TsO-CD) is considered an important cyclodextrin (CD) intermediate for easy introduction of various functional groups such as halides, azide, amine and imidazole groups to afford functionalized CD derivatives. This protocol describes the use 1-(p-tosyl)-imidazole as the precursor to react with β-cyclodextrin, affording highly pure mono-substituted product with an acceptable yield.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"pe2gw","text":"Mono-6-tosyl-β-cyclodextrin (TsO-CD) is considered as an important cyclodextrin (CD) intermediate for easy introduction of various functional groups such as halides, azide, amine and imidazole groups to afford functionalized CD derivatives1,2,3. This is especially so for mono-azido-CD which could serve as a precursor of Cu (I) catalytic 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition (click chemistry)4,5. However, the synthesis of TsO-CD often results in randomly substituted products, of which the separation of pure mono-substituted products can be difficult due to their similar physical properties. It is therefore attractive to use 1-(p-tosyl)-imidazole as the precursor to react with β-cyclodextrin, affording highly pure mono-substituted product with an acceptable yield6. This reaction is described in this protocol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":239,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":382,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":759,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Add 300 mL dichloromethane into a 2 L erlenmeyer flask containing a Teflon-coated magnetic stirrer bar.
Add 25.5 g imidazole (2) and 75 g p-toluenesulfonyl chloride (1) into the erlenmeyer flask with stirring.
Dissolve 39 g sodium bicarbonate in 500 mL DI water to form a solution.
Add the solution obtained in Step 3 into the erlenmeyer flask.
Add 5.6 mL triethylamine into the flask as the phase transfer catalyst.
Stir the reaction mixture for 12 h at room temperature.
Separate the dichloromethane layer from the aqueous layer.
Dry the dichloromethane using anhydrous magnesium sulfate.
Concentrate the dichloromethane to about 125 mL on a rotary evaporator.
Add 25 mL hexane into the resulted dichloromethane.
Allow the residual solution to stand overnight to form white crystals.
Add 450 mL DI water into a 1 L round bottomed flask containing a Teflon-coated magnetic stirrer bar.
Add 20 g dry β-CD into the flask with stirring.
Heat the mixture to 60 °C to form a clear solution.
Cool the solution to room temperature.
Add 15.5 g of 1-(p-tosyl)-imidazole (3) fine powder into the solution.
Stir the reaction mixture for 2 h.
Add a solution of 9 g sodium hydroxide in 25 mL DI water into the reaction solution after 20 min using a pressure-equalizing addition funnel.
Add 24 g ammonium chloride into the reaction solution with swirling to dissolve all the solids.
Concentrate the solution to half of its original volume by blowing a stream of air overnight.
Filter the reaction mixture and collect the white solid.
Wash the white solid using 100 mL ice water twice and then 200 mL acetone.
Dry the crude product under vacuum over calcium chloride to constant weight.
Low yield of (3): Concentrate the dichloromethane to below 125 mL. Low yield of (4): Repeat reaction with highly dried β-CD. Grind the 1-(p-tosyl)-imidazole into powder as fine as possible in Step 5.
Synthesis of (3): About 20 h. Synthesis of (4) : About 24 h including the drying step.
The authors declare no competing financial interests

Click chemistry for facile immobilization of cyclodextrin derivatives onto silica as chiral stationary phases by Yong Wang, Yin Xiao, Timothy Thatt Yang Tan, and Siu-Choon Ng published in Tetrahedron Letters on 21 Feb, 2011 https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.tetlet.2008.06.048 A reliable synthesis of 2- and 6-amino-β-cyclodextrin and permethylated-β-cyclodextrin by I. Wayan Muderawan, Teng Teng Ong, Teck Chia Lee, David J. Young, Chi Bun Ching, and Siu Choon Ng published in Tetrahedron Letters on 21 Feb, 2011 https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.tetlet.2005.09.099 Enantioseparation of a novel “click” chemistry derived native β-cyclodextrin chiral stationary phase for high-performance liquid chromatography by Yong Wang, Teng-Teng Ong, Lai-Sheng Li, Timothy Thatt Yang Tan and Siu-Choon Ng published in Journal Of Chromatography A on 21 Feb, 2011 https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.chroma.2009.01.039 Facile synthesis of mono-6-amino-6-deoxy-α-, β-, γ-cyclodextrin hydrochlorides for molecular recognition, chiral separation and drug delivery by Weihua Tang and Siu-Choon Ng published in Nature Protocols on 21 Feb, 2011 https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/nprot.2008.37 Preparation of cyclodextrin chiral stationary phases by organic soluble catalytic 'click' chemistry by Yong Wang, Hui Chen, Yin Xiao, Cheong Hengq Ng, Ting Shan Oh, Timothy Thatt Yang Tan, and Siu Choon Ng published in Nature Protocols on 10 Jun, 2011 https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/nprot.2011.340
Figure 1. Scheme Synthesis of 1-(p-tosyl)-imidazole and mono-6-tosyl-CD
